GCC LOGISTICS INTELLIGENCE PLATFORM
Methodology
Our signals and indicators are built on a rigorous quantitative framework. We apply advanced mathematical and statistical models — including queuing theory, linear and logarithmic regressions, and multivariate predictive models — trained on historical trade, freight, and maritime data. Each signal is validated through causal chain analysis and stress-tested across geopolitical and macroeconomic scenarios to ensure decision-grade reliability.
GCC LOGISTICS INTELLIGENCE PLATFORM
Data Sources
● All data used is publicly available
We do not use proprietary or commercially licensed data feeds. All underlying data is sourced from public international bodies, open maritime databases, and published government statistics — then processed, aggregated and modelled by our analytical team.
MARITIME & VESSEL TRAFFIC
AIS (Automatic Identification System) open data · Kpler satellite & AIS aggregates · Lloyd's List vessel movements · MarineTraffic public vessel registry
FREIGHT RATES & TRADE FLOWS
Freightos Baltic Index (FBX) · Shanghai Containerized Freight Index (SCFI) · World Container Index (WCI) · UNCTAD Review of Maritime Transport · World Bank trade statistics
PORT OPERATIONS & INFRASTRUCTURE
GCC port authority public statistics (Mwani Qatar, Oman Ports, ACWA, SRCA) · World Bank Logistics Performance Index · UNCTAD Port Monitor · MDS Transmodal public reports
GEOPOLITICAL & RISK
Lloyd's Market Association (LMA) war risk designations · IMO circulars & safety advisories · UN Security Council resolutions · Publicly reported insurance premium indices
MACROECONOMIC & ENERGY
IMF World Economic Outlook · EIA (U.S. Energy Information Administration) · OPEC Monthly Oil Market Report · World Bank Commodity Markets Outlook

Strait of Hormuz — 14-Day Transit Monitor
Jun 29–Jul 12 2026 · IMF PortWatch (Oxford/UN Global Pulse AIS) · baseline ~88/day commercial vessels · dark fleet partially excluded · auto-updated daily
IMF PortWatch · straits.live/api/v1/transits · Oxford / UN Global Pulsedata to jul 12
⚠ DATA LIMITATION — AIS GAPS & DARK FLEET
IMF PortWatch counts AIS-broadcasting commercial vessels only (Oxford/UN Global Pulse methodology). Vessels operating without transponders (dark fleet) are not captured — independent satellite synthesis estimates true throughput may run 1.3–1.8× higher than AIS-visible counts during periods of elevated dark-fleet activity. The Jun 24 peak of 52 AIS-visible transits likely understates actual flow by this margin. Kpler (satellite + AIS synthesis) remains the most credible independent cross-check — kpler.com ↗.

ALERT
IRGC enforces all-corridor control since Jun 25 southern-route crackdown. Second merchant vessel struck Jun 27 — IMO crew-evacuation plans paused. MoU 60-day window under stress; nuclear talks postponed. Traffic peaked at 49 transits Jun 24 (Windward) before reverting. JWC war-risk listing extended through Jun 30+. Lloyd's List ↗

War-Risk Insurance
▲ +1.2pp post-Jun 27 strike • re-escalating from 2% MoU floor
Brent at $88.10 (Jul 19 2026). +22% above $72 pre-crisis baseline — crisis premium sustained as Jun 27 second vessel strike reversed MoU-driven relief rally. Western carrier exclusion keeping Gulf supply uncertainty elevated.
War-risk premiums halved from ~5% to ~2% hull value after Jun 17 MoU. Jun 27 second vessel strike drove re-escalation to ~3.2% (+1.2pp). Lloyd's Chubb-led consortium (Jun 19) adds $200M hull + $200M cargo capacity — capacity now under pressure.

MODEL v1.5.2  ·  Dwell = Tvessel + Tyard  ·  Tvessel = M/M/c queuing proxy: Ts + min(W, B) × Ts / B, capped at 2×Ts  ·  Tyard = Tcust × Γα, OLS power-law in log-space (Γ = congestion ratio vs pre-crisis throughput; α, Tcust calibrated per port)  ·  CI = max(RMSE × 1.5, 8%) applied as ±% of midpoint
Sources: K+N advisory Jul 15  ·  Xeneta congestion index  ·  Vizion API dwell telemetry  ·  Inchcape port reports  ·  Portcast Jul 18  ·  Mawani / OPAZ / APM Terminals

FLEET & OPERATOR SIGNALS · Capacity changes since Hormuz closure · Jun 2026
TruKKer (UAE) — scaling 60 to 500 moves/day. Fixed transparent rates from Khorfakkan activated.
Flow Progressive (SA) — fleet 340 to 500+ 40ft trailers since conflict began.
Saudi TGA — eased GCC refrigerated truck entry rules March 2026. Hwy 95 goods tripled to $830M.
Saudi Landbridge — Jeddah–Dammam rail ($7B, 1,300 km). Projected $4.2B/yr transport cost savings.

△ Model Validation — Red Sea Crisis Backtest (2023–2024)
Regression model run against 4 known periods of the Houthi / Red Sea disruption • Actual: Freightos Baltic FBX01 (Shanghai–N.Europe $/FEU) • Base: $1,400 (Q3 2023 market low)
Period Brent Util Extra NM War× Cong. Predicted Actual FBX01 Error
◆ Coefficient Optimizer
Grid search over ~23,500 combinations • Pass 1: best linear fit • Pass 2: non-linear β₂ power law • runs in <10ms
⚠ Key Model Limitation Identified
The linear β₂ (capacity utilisation) term systematically under-predicts peak-crisis rates by 15–25%. Real markets exhibit a convex / near-logistic rate curve as utilisation approaches 95–100% — the linear approximation diverges sharply at those extremes. Kalouptsidi (2014, AER) documents this non-linearity in bulk shipping; the same dynamic applies to container markets. A log-linear specification or a logistic utilisation term would reduce peak RMSE from ~14% to an estimated ~6–8%.
Literature: Notteboom & Vernimmen (2009) J.Transport Geography (bunker β) • Kalouptsidi (2014) AER (capacity elasticity) • IMF WP/24/058 “Sailing into Headwinds: The Red Sea Disruption” (detour & war-risk) • Drewry Port Performance Index 2021–22 (congestion pass-through) • Hummels & Schaur (2013) AER (time-value in trade)
FBX01 actuals: Freightos Baltic Index · Conditions: market consensus estimatesbacktested

KEY INSIGHTS – GCC COUNTRY EXPOSURE
Baseline data — crude oil / Hormuz active disruption scenario.
ⓘ Methodology, Data Sources & Assumptions
▼ Show
◆ Distance & Transit Time
Route distances sourced from Sea-Distances.org and MarineTraffic port-to-port calculators for standard maritime routes.
Additional Time (days) =
Extra Distance (nm) ÷ (Vessel Speed (kt) × 24)
Fleet-average speeds: VLCC 13 kt • LNG carrier 19 kt • Capesize bulk 14 kt • Panamax 14 kt • Container 20 kt
Source: Clarksons Research, MarineTraffic average voyage speed data 2023
◆ Transport Cost Multiplier
Uses the same documented regression model as the Scenario Simulator tab. The distance coefficient β₃ is validated against observed freight rate data.
Cost Multiplier = max(0.5,
  1 + 0.009×(Brent−72)
  + 0.045×(ExtraNm÷100)
  + 0.050×(Utilisation−72))

β₃ = 0.045 per 100 nm • Base Brent: $72/bbl
Utilisation held at 72% baseline
Regression: Stopford (2009) Maritime Economics; validated against FBX01 vs distance spread 2019–2023 (UNCTAD data)
◆ Price Impact Methodology
Method A — Historical Event References
Documented outcomes from real disruption events. These are observed data, not predictions.
Method B — Elasticity-Based
ΔP = (ΔQ ÷ Q) × (1 ÷ εd) × P

ΔQ = disrupted % of market
εd = short-run demand elasticity
P = baseline commodity price
Elasticities: crude oil −0.05 (Hamilton 2009); LNG −0.10 (Wood Mackenzie); agricultural −0.15 to −0.25 (IMF 2022)
◆ Volume Data Sources
Crude Oil / Petroleum: EIA Chokepoints 2022; IEA OMR 2023
LNG: GIIGNL Annual Report 2024
Wheat / Corn / Rice / Soybeans: USDA FAS MY2022/23
Copper: ICSG Copper Bulletin 2023
Iron Ore / Coal: Clarksons Shipping Review 2023
Nickel: INSG / NiPERA 2023
Rare Earths: USGS Mineral Commodity Summaries 2023
Semiconductors / EV Batteries: WSTS / BloombergNEF 2023
◆ Routing Assumptions
Primary routes based on AIS vessel density data (MarineTraffic 2023). Alternative routes are the next-best viable commercial option per vessel type.

VLCC (crude, iron ore): Cannot transit Panama; Suez at reduced draft. No bypass for Hormuz — product redistribution via Fujairah anchorage only.

LNG Carriers >80,000 DWT: Cannot use most secondary straits. Hormuz closure requires US Gulf supply substitution (significantly higher transit distances to Asia).
◆ What This Module Does NOT Do
This module displays observed data and transparent calculations only. It does NOT:
• Generate AI predictions or confidence scores
• Assign vulnerability or resilience scores
• Account for demand destruction under prolonged disruptions
• Model strategic reserve drawdowns
• Predict geopolitical responses or supply substitution
• Distinguish spot vs. contract freight markets

All outputs are mechanical calculations based on documented volumes and distances, fully auditable against the stated sources.

ⓘ  Port of NEOM — Under Calibration
No commercial liner rates available yet
Port of NEOM has no active commercial liner services operating at scale today. The port is under phased commissioning as part of the NEOM megaproject, and no verified spot-market data is available from Freightos, Xeneta, or Drewry for this lane.

We are actively working to calibrate freight estimates as carrier loops are announced and the port opens to commercial traffic. Rates will be published here once reliable data becomes available.
What we know:
•  Location: Gulf of Aqaba, northwest Saudi Arabia (lat 28.2°N)
•  Routing: Red Sea / Suez — unaffected by Hormuz closure
•  SHA transit estimate: ~24 days  •  HAM transit estimate: ~8 days
